en: Let's go to literature, because we didn't really mention literature much, except you did briefly mention Epic of Gilgamesh.
ko: 문학 얘기를 하죠. 길가메시 서사시를 잠깐 언급하셨었는데요.

en: So that was written in cuneiform, it's one of the earliest works of literature. That's right. Can you tell me about this work?
ko: 설형문자로 쓰인 가장 초기 문학 작품 중 하나죠. 맞아요. 이 작품에 대해 말씀해주세요.

en: Yeah. Well, we know it best from this Assyrian library set of tablets, there are 12 of them.
ko: 네. 아시리아 도서관의 토판 세트로 가장 잘 알려져 있어요. 12개 토판이고 꽤 길어요. 길가메시가 주인공이에요.

en: It's a 12-tablet work, so it's quite long, and Gilgamesh is the hero of it.
ko: 더 이른 텍스트에서도 알고 있고 길가메시는 실존 인물이었어요.

en: But the literature, we know it from earlier texts, and we know that Gilgamesh lived.
ko: 우루크의 왕이었고 알렉산더처럼 사후에도 세상에 살아있는 사람이었어요.

en: He was a real person, he was a king in Uruk, and he was one of those people who lived after their death in the world, like Alexander, for example.
ko: 살아있을 때 이야기가 있었고 사후에도 이야기가 있었어요. 처음에는 구전 문학이었고 1800년대쯤 수메르어나 바빌로니아어로 기록되기 시작했어요.

en: So there were stories about Gilgamesh when he was alive, there were stories about him afterwards, and firstly they were oral literature, not written down at all, and then around the 1800s, people started to write them down in Sumerian or Babylonian.
ko: 작품들이 모여서 결국 길가메시의 모험에 대한 호메로스식의 긴 12토판 작품으로 엮어졌어요.

en: So there was a corpus, and eventually they were woven into this long 12 Homeric type thing about the adventures of Gilgamesh.
ko: 확실히 문학이에요. 인간성과 불멸, 신의 손아귀에 있는 인간에 관한 것이고 흥미진진한 이야기가 많아요.

en: So it is certainly literature, and it's to do with humanity and immortality and man in the hands of the gods, and incorporates lots of interesting, exciting stories.
ko: 매우 할리우드적이에요. 세련된 니느웨 판본에서도 구전 문학의 뿌리가 보여요.

en: It's a very Hollywood-y kind of thing, and you can see within it, even in the sophisticated Nineveh version, its roots are in oral literature.
ko: 누가 말하면 '길가메시가 입을 열어 친구 엔키두에게 말했다'고 나오고 그 다음 대사가 나와요.

en: Because when somebody speaks, it says Gilgamesh opened his mouth to speak and addressed his friend Enkidu, and then there's a speech, and then Enkidu opened his mouth and addressed his friend Gilgamesh.
ko: 읽을 때는 그런 게 필요 없어요. 구전 연기 때 화자가 말한 게 텍스트에 그대로 남은 거예요.

en: Well, when you're reading a story, you don't need that, and that must be because when there was an enacting of an oral thing, a narrator would say, and it suddenly got frozen into the text.
ko: 읽으면 이상해요. 한 사람이 말하고 다른 사람이 말하는 건 당연한데 항상 이 복잡한 문구가 끼어 있어요.

en: So it's a very strange thing, because if you're reading it, it's obvious that one person speaks and the other person speaks, and they always have this complicated thing stuck in the text.
ko: 주인공들이 시대를 초월한 이야기를 연기하면 기록자가 대본처럼 길가메시가 말했다고 쓴 거겠죠.

en: So it must be an echo of presumably you have your protagonists enacting their timeless matter, and the person who's writing it down says, and then Gilgamesh said, like in a script.
ko: 그 시대에 문자로 이야기를 하는 것에 대해 뭘 말할 수 있나요?

en: I mean, what can you say about the telling of stories in written form during that time?
ko: 시간을 거슬러 올라간다고 보세요? 네. 모닥불가의 이야기 전통이요.

en: Do you think that too stretched back in time? I do. I think the fireside narrative matter.
ko: 우리가 어릴 때는 휴가 때 모닥불 주위에서 기타 치고 놀았지만, 사람들이 어둠 뒤에 모여 불 옆에서 이야기하는 환경이 인간에게 자연스럽게 서사가 꽃피는 곳이에요.

en: When we were kids, it would be twerks with a guitar sitting around a fire on holiday, but that mechanism when people gather after dark when there is a fire and talk is the sort of environment where narrative accounts flourish naturally among human beings.
ko: 이야기, 이야기 전하기. 실용적일 필요 없어요. 어떤 면에서 문학적일 수 있어요.

en: Stories, telling a story. It doesn't have to be pragmatic. It can be literary in a way.
ko: 길가메시 같은 인물이거나, 신들에 관한 이야기거나, 누군가 은하수를 보고 신이 전차를 타고 올라가는 거라고 상상하는 거예요.

en: Yeah, either a human person like Gilgamesh, or stories about the gods and someone sees the Milky Way and they think it's a god riding a chariot up it, and then they have a story about it, and all those sorts of things, or whatever it would be.
ko: 호모 사피엔스에게서 강한 창작 원칙이 표면화되는 걸 인정해야 해요. 달리는 영양을 색으로 벽에 그려보세요.

en: But I think probably you have to allow for a strong creative principle surfacing in Homo You try drawing a running antelope in color on a wall.
ko: 기술의 질, 예술적 능력이 최고 수준이에요.

en: I mean, the quality of the workmanship, of the artistic ability, is unsurpassable.
ko: 좋은 정도가 아니에요. 이 초기 시대에 어떻게 설명할 수 있죠?

en: It's not just good. So how is that an explicable thing at this very early date?
ko: 모든 인구 중에 바보도 있고 아인슈타인도 있고, 어딘가에 렘브란트가 있다는 뜻이에요.

en: It means that among all the population, you have imbeciles and Einsteins, and somewhere along the line, you have Rembrandts.
ko: 유럽 동굴 벽화의 절반은 한 사람이 그렸을 수 있어요. 모든 가정에 천재 화가가 있었을 리 없잖아요.

en: And I imagine that half the cave paintings in Europe were done by one person. I mean, you get the impression every family had a genius painter.
ko: 돌아다니며 그림을 그리는 사람이 있었을 거예요. 바로 정확하게 그릴 수 있었으니까.

en: It's impossible. Probably there was a person who went from place to place doing these paintings because they were so... You could draw straight away accurately like that.
ko: 창의적 예술 능력과 기술의 결정체예요.

en: But they are a distillation of creative artistic ability plus skill.
ko: 이건 꽤 초기 단계잖아요, 동굴 벽화가?

en: So this is right at the pretty early stage, is it not, the cave painting material?
ko: 그런 아이디어를 담은 인간의 자원을 고려해야 해요.

en: So if you consider the human stock which encapsulates such ideas ever after, then you have to reckon with that.
ko: 고려해야 해요. 매우 창의적인 사람들이었어요.

en: You have to reckon with that. Very creative people.
ko: 젊은이들에게 유명한 전투, 홍수, 불 만드는 법, 바퀴 발명에 대해 알려주는 기능의 이야기들이요.

en: So the function of stories to tell the young about what happened and about famous battles, when the flood came, or how people learned to make fire, or how we invented the wheel, all those sorts of things everybody puts down.
ko: 그게 아마 실제로 일어난 일이에요. 자기 종족 중 놀라운 능력을 가진 사람을 존경하는 능력도 있었고요.

en: But that's presumably what absolutely happened. And you have the capacity for people to adore and to respect, among their own kind, people of astounding ability.
ko: 엄청나게 빠른 사냥꾼이나 북극곰과 씨름하는 사람도 있었을 거예요.

en: There must have been hunters who were ferociously quick and, you know, wrestle with polar bears and all that kind of thing.
ko: 이 모든 게 이야기꾼의 재료가 돼요.

en: And all this stuff will be grist to the narrators.
ko: 일이 복잡해지고 정교해지면서 교훈이 포함되거나 의도치 않게 교훈이 나오기도 했어요.

en: And as things got more complicated and more sophisticated, so lessons might be incorporated or lessons might come out of them unintentionally.
ko: 교훈 없는 이야기는 생각해보면 보통 비도덕적이니까요.

en: Because if you tell a story without a moral, it is usually immoral if you think about it.
ko: 그리고 그 많은 이야기가 안타깝게도 시간 속에 사라졌거나 아직 발견되지 않았어요.

en: And many of those stories are sadly lost to time or not yet found.
ko: 그리고 그 많은 이야기가 안타깝게도 시간 속에 사라졌어요.
